answer:
**In "The Wanderer" (ch. 11 of *Old English Elegies*), the speaker—a lone exile deprived of his retainers and homeland—refers to his lost human lord/protector with three compound Old English terms: *goldwine* ("gold-friend," i.e., generous lord), *winedryhten* ("friend-lord"), and *mondryhten* ("liege-lord" or "protector of [his] man").**[^1][^2][^3]

These appear in his lamenting monologue (lines 20–23, 36–38, 40), e.g.:

> siþþan geara iu **goldwine** minne  
> hrusan heolstre biwrah  
> ...  
> his **winedryhtnes** leofes larcwidum  
> ...  
> þæt he his **mondryhten** clyppe ond cysse.[^4]

Among these, ***mondryhten*** most precisely evokes "protector," as *mon-* ("man") implies the retainer's sworn protector, and the passage describes (in dream) embracing/kissing him "on cneo lecge honda ond heafod" (laying hands and head on knee).[^4] The translation renders it "liege-lord," capturing the feudal bond.[^5]

He also notes having "few beloved protectors" (*lyt hafað leofra geholena*), using *geholena* (guardians/helpers) in plural for such figures generally.[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), original variant, [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/original/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/original/#p-9462cc) (main speech; *goldwine* at l. 23).

[^3]: Ibid., *mondryhten* (l. 40), *winedryhtnes* (genitive, l. 36–38).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, translation variant, [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^5]: Ibid. (whole speech). 

*(Note: Paragraph IDs align across variants; chapter has 3 logical paragraphs in translation, with the core lament in the middle. No single non-compound *dryhten* or *hlaford* names the human lord—*dryhten* elsewhere denotes God.)*
